content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Michael J. Maurer 
Best Best & Krieger LLP 
300 South Grand Avenue, 25th Floor 
Los Angeles, CA 90071 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-182(a) 
Dear Mr. Maurer: 
September 14, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Lafayette City Council Members 
Mark Mitchell, Mike Anderson, Brandt Anderson, Tracy Reilly, and Don Tatzin regarding their 
duties under the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note the following: 
• This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not 
act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (/11 re Oglesby ( I 975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) 
• The Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) 
Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have 
already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective 
actions. 
• Finally, please note that we are only providing advice under the Act and not under other 
general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 
1090. 
FACTS 
The City's Municipal Code contains a three-stage approval process for projects requiring 
design review within certain zones, including residential zones. The project is first reviewed by the 
Design Review Commission; decisions are then appealable to the Planning Commission and then 
the City Council. 
A member of the City's Planning Commission worked in his private capacity as a licensed 
architect. This Planning Commissioner was hired by a citizen of Lafayette to design a "cabana," an 
accessory structure on residential property. Construction of the design required various city 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califpmia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-l 6-l 82(a) 
Page No. 2 
approvals. In his professional capacity, the Planning Commissioner submitted the application to the 
City's Planning Department. Planning staff processed the application and presented it to the Design 
Review Commission (DRC), a City commission that comments on design and architectural issues. 
At one presentation to the DRC, the Planning Commissioner served as the Planning Commission 
liaison to the DRC and stepped down from the dais to present the application to the DRC. 
After several meetings, the DRC approved the project. A citizen then appealed the approval 
to the Planning Commission. Staff presented the application to the Planning Commission. At the 
Planning Commission meeting, the Planning Commissioner acknowledged the conflict on the 
record, recused himself from all participation, left the meeting room and did not vote on the 
Planning Commission's approval of the application. 
A citizen then appealed the Planning Commission's approval to the City Council, and the 
Planning Commissioner resigned from the Planning Commission (the notice of appeal and the letter 
of resignation were received on the same day.) Even though the Planning Commissioner has 
resigned, he has not presented the application to the City Council nor has he been present at any 
City Council meetings related to the application. For appeals to the City Council, the Municipal 
Code, section 6-2 33(c), states: 
"De Novo Hearing. The city council shall hear the appeal as a new matter. 
The original applicant has the burden of proof. The city council may act upon the 
application, either brranting it, conditionally granting it or denying it, irrespective 
of the precise details of the appeal. In addition to considering the testimony and 
evidence presented at the hearing on the appeal, the city council shall consider all 
pertinent information from the file as a result of the previous hearings from which 
the appeal is taken." (Emphasis added.) 
Thus, the City Council may consider information from the file, but ultimately the City 
Council is considering the application anew. You noted that the applicant is entitled to an appeal 
hearing within 6 0  days. 
For purposes of the letter you ask that we assume the following: 
• None of the City Council members have any disqualifying conflicts of interest related to the 
application. 
• That there was in fact improper participation by the Planning Commissioner during a prior 
stage. 
• You also note that the property that is the subject of the application is owned by a private 
property owner, and the City Council has no reason to believe that the property owner 
engaged in any wrongdoing. 


QUESTIONS, ANALYSIS AND CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-l 6-l 82(a ) 
Page No. 3 
1. May tl,e 11amed City Co1111cil members vote 011 a develop111e11t applicatio11 tl,at tl,e City 
Co1111cil reviews de 11ovo if a Pla1111i11g Com111issio11er wit/, a co11flict of illlerest participated i11 a 
prior stage of tl,e application? 
Section 8710 0 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
attempting to influence a governmental decision, in which the official has a financial interest. This 
provision prohibits councilmembers from participating in a decision of the city council in which 
they have a financial interest. Generally, however, the Act's conflict-of-interest requirements apply 
to individual officials, not public bodies. Thus, it is the responsibility of each public official to 
perform his or her duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by the official's own 
financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported the official. 
Moreover, Section 87 l 0 0  does not impose liability on a councilmember for making or 
participating in making a decision in which another official has a conflict of interest. Thus, the 
council members would not have a conflict of interest nor would they otherwise be prohibited from 
voting on a decision in which a planning commissioner, with a conflict of interest, participated. 
(Benjamin Advice Letter, No. A-86-148.) 
The Act does provide that it is a violation of the Act for any person to purposely or 
negligently cause any other person to violate the Act, or to aid and abet any other person in the 
violation of the Act. Absent aiding or abetting another person in violating the provision of the title, 
members are not responsible for the conduct of other members. ( Willoughby Advice Letter, No. 1-
91-429.) However, in the Leahy Advice Letter, No. A-95- 038, we advised that mere participation in 
the decision would not constitute aiding and abetting the official with the conflict of interest under 
the Act. (See also, Kloeker Advice Letter, No. A-95- 0 18. ) Accordingly, the Act's contlict-of­
interest provisions do not preclude the city council from deliberating and voting on the matter. 
You stated that in making your request, you were aware of Section 9 10 0 3. That section 
provides: 
"Upon a preliminary showing in an action brought by a person residing in 
the jurisdiction that a violation of Article I ( commencing with Section 871 0 0 ), 
Article 4 (commencing with Section 874 0 0 ), or Article 4.5 (commencing with 
Section 8745 0 )  of Chapter 7 of this title or of a disqualification provision of a 
Conflict of Interest Code has occurred, the court may restrain the execution of any 
official action in relation to which such a violation occurred, pending final 
adjudication. If it is ultimately determined that a violation has occurred and that 
the official action might not otherwise have been taken or approved, the court 
may set the official action aside as void. The official actions covered by this 
subsection include, but are not limited to orders, permits, resolutions and 
contracts .... " 
You stated that you were not seeking advice as to whether or how a court would remedy an action if 
it determined there to be a conflict of interest. Thus, we do not discuss this provision further. 


File No. A-16-l 82(a) 
Page No. 4 
2. lftl,e members oft/re City Co1111cil believe that a City official may /rave participated in 
a decision de.\pite /raving a co11jlict of i11terest, do tire members /rave a legal obligatio11 to report 
tire matter to tire FPPC for i11vestigatio11? Would i11divid11al Council members he subject to fines, 
pe11alties, or otlrer sa11ctio11s if a pote11tial cm,jlict of i11terest i.'i 11ot reported to tire FPPC for 
i11vestigatio11? 
You also ask whether your councilmembers have a duty to report apparent violations of the 
Act. The Act imposes a specific duty upon some officers but does not impose such duties on city 
council members. 2 Thus, the council members would not be subject to fines, penalties, or other 
sanctions if a potential conflict of interest is not reported. (See, Leahy Advice Letter, No. A-95-
038.) However, please note that although the Act docs not impose a duty to report apparent 
violations, it would be in the public interest to report violations of the Act. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
General Counsel 
JWW:jgl 
c For example, Section 810 IO pro\•tdes specifically with respect to reports and statements filed with a "filing 
officer," the filing officer shall: "Report apparent v1ola11ons of thts title to the appropriate agencies .... " Regulation 
18 I I 5(a)(6) clarifies that an apparent violation exists when: "(A) The filing officer knows or has reason to believe that 
the statement contains material inaccuracies or omissions; (B) A filer fails to file all or part of h1s statement or refuses 
to file all or part of his statement after reasonable notice has been provided by the filing officer."
